subject: Chemistry
title: Antibiotic Resistance: Evolution Fights Back
description: A 60-second lesson on Antibiotic Resistance: Evolution Fights Back.

hook.text: Bacteria are evolving faster than we can make antibiotics. Drug-resistant infections kill 1.27 million people yearly.

buildup.text: When antibiotics kill 99.9% of bacteria, the 0.1% that survive carry resistance genes. They multiply and spread.

discovery.text: Bacteria share resistance genes through plasmids—tiny DNA rings passed between cells, even between different species.

twist.text: MRSA (methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus) resists most antibiotics. It kills more Americans than HIV each year.

climax.text: Fleming warned in his Nobel speech that misuse would breed resistance. We didn't listen. The post-antibiotic era is approaching.

punchline.text: Evolution outpaces chemistry. Every antibiotic creates pressure for resistance.

quiz.question: How do bacteria spread antibiotic resistance between species?
options: Through plasmids—small DNA rings transferred between bacterial cells
By releasing resistance chemicals into the surrounding environment
Through mutations that occur only during antibiotic treatment
